metadata.summary: Semantic versioning, version constraint syntax, CHANGELOG maintenance, and registry publication checklist for Terraform modules used by multiple consumers.

content.title: Terraform Module Versioning
content.summary: Published Terraform modules MUST use Semantic Versioning (SemVer) with Git tags, version constraints on every module source reference, and a maintained CHANGELOG.md. Without pinned version constraints, a module update can silently break all consumers on the next terraform init.

why.p: Terraform resolves module versions at init time. An unpinned module source (no version argument) fetches the latest commit on the default branch. If a breaking change is merged overnight, every pipeline that runs terraform init the next day picks it up — often during a production apply. Pinned version constraints make upgrades explicit and reviewable. SemVer communicates the impact of a release to consumers without requiring them to read every diff.

list.item: Authoring a module consumed by more than one caller (team-shared or registry module).
Upgrading a module dependency — need to understand what constraint syntax is safe.
Releasing a new module version — need the correct tag and CHANGELOG format.
Setting up a new consumer module call — choosing the right version constraint operator.
Private local modules referenced by relative path (./modules/vpc) in a mono-repo — they have no separate release lifecycle.
Single-team repos where the module author and all callers commit in the same branch — version pinning adds overhead without benefit.

section.name: semver
section.p: SemVer defines three version segments: MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH. For Terraform modules, the key distinction is whether a change requires consumers to update their module call.

rule.statement: Major version bumps (X.0.0) MUST be used for any change that requires consumers to update variable names, remove previously supported variables, or change output names. A migration guide MUST accompany every major release.
rule.rationale: Without a clear signal, consumers cannot distinguish a bug fix from a breaking change at a glance. Major bumps give teams a predictable gate for planning upgrades.

rule.statement: Minor version bumps (0.X.0) MUST be used for new optional variables, new outputs, or new resources that are backward-compatible. Existing callers MUST continue to work without modification.
rule.rationale: Consumers who rely on ~> constraints (see below) will pick up minor versions automatically. Breaking backward compatibility in a minor bump violates consumer expectations.

rule.statement: Patch version bumps (0.0.X) MUST be used for bug fixes that do not add new features or change behavior. No consumer code changes required.
rule.rationale: Patch bumps signal safe auto-adoption for consumers using pessimistic constraints.

example.title: SemVer version decision table
example.code: Change → Version bump ---------------------------------------------------------- Rename variable `enable_nat` → `nat_enabled` → MAJOR (2.0.0) Remove deprecated output `subnet_id` → MAJOR (2.0.0) Add new optional variable `enable_ipv6` → MINOR (1.1.0) Add new output `ipv6_cidr_block` → MINOR (1.1.0) Fix NAT gateway depends_on race condition → PATCH (1.0.1) Update default instance type for cost → PATCH (1.0.1)

section.name: version-constraints
section.p: The version argument on a module source controls which releases Terraform will accept. Using the wrong operator is the most common cause of accidental breaking-change adoption.

rule.statement: All module source references that include a version argument MUST use the pessimistic constraint operator (~>) to allow patch and minor updates within a major version, or an explicit range. The exact-pin operator (=) MUST only be used when a specific patch is known to fix a critical bug.
rule.rationale: ~> 5.0 allows 5.0.1, 5.1.0, 5.9.9 but blocks 6.0.0 — safe for routine updates while blocking breaking changes. Exact pins require manual bumps for every patch release, increasing maintenance burden.

rule.statement: Registry and GitHub module sources MUST always include the version argument. Local path sources (./modules/X) MUST NOT include a version argument.
rule.rationale: Local modules have no separate release lifecycle — the version argument is ignored and its presence misleads maintainers.

example.title: Version constraint examples
Unpinned module source
example.note: Any breaking change merged to the upstream module HEAD will be adopted silently on the next terraform init, breaking the next plan or apply.

section.name: changelog
section.p: The CHANGELOG.md is the consumer-facing release log. It must be maintained as part of every release, not retroactively.

rule.statement: Every published module MUST maintain a CHANGELOG.md in the module root. Each release MUST document: the version number, release date, and categorized changes (Added, Changed, Breaking Changes, Fixed, Deprecated, Removed). Breaking changes MUST be in their own section and include migration instructions.
rule.rationale: Consumers deciding whether to upgrade need to evaluate impact without reading the full diff. A categorized CHANGELOG reduces the upgrade decision from O(diff) to O(CHANGELOG section).

example.title: CHANGELOG.md format
example.code:
# Changelog
## [2.1.0] - 2025-01-15 ### Added - Support for IPv6 CIDR blocks - New output `ipv6_cidr_block`
### Changed - Default instance type updated to t3.medium
## [2.0.0] - 2024-11-01 ### Breaking Changes - Renamed variable `enable_nat` to `nat_gateway_enabled` - Removed deprecated `single_nat_gateway` variable
### Migration Replace in all module calls: enable_nat = true → nat_gateway_enabled = true Remove any `single_nat_gateway` references.

section.name: release-checklist
section.p: A release checklist prevents common omissions that break the registry sync or leave consumers without upgrade guidance.

checklist.check: Version follows SemVer — verify MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH classification against the change table.
CHANGELOG.md updated with new version section before tagging.
Breaking changes documented with migration guide (required for MAJOR bumps).
README includes updated usage example matching new variable names.
Git tag created with vX.Y.Z format (e.g., v2.1.0).
Registry sync verified after tag push (Terraform Registry auto-syncs from GitHub tags).
Team notified of breaking changes (major releases require consumer team awareness).
Internal consumer modules updated if managed in the same repo.
